# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Collective Bargaining. Mention any two features.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Collective Bargaining is the process of negotiation between an employer and a group of employees (represented by a trade union) to determine the conditions of employment, including wages, working hours, benefits, and workplace policies.

**Two Features:**
1. It is a **group process** involving a union representing workers, not individual negotiation.
2. It results in a **Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA)** that is a legally binding contract.

---

### Question 2: What is a Grievance? List any two causes of grievance.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Grievance** is any real or perceived feeling of personal injustice experienced by an employee in connection with their employment. As per Edwin Flippo, it is *"any discontent or dissatisfaction, whether expressed or not, whether valid or not, arising out of anything connected with the company."*

**Two Causes:**
1. **Wage-related causes** — unfair deductions, pay cuts, irregular salary payment.
2. **Supervisor-related causes** — favoritism, harassment, rude behaviour, unfair work allocation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Trade Union. Discuss its main objectives.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **Trade Union** is a continuous, voluntary association of wage earners formed to protect and promote their economic, social, and vocational interests through collective action. It is recognized under the **Trade Unions Act, 1926**.

**Features:**
- Voluntary, continuous, democratic, service-oriented, based on collective strength.

**Main Objectives:**
1. **Wage and Salary Negotiation** — Securing fair remuneration through collective bargaining.
2. **Better Working Conditions** — Safety, health, reasonable hours of work.
3. **Job Security** — Protection against arbitrary termination.
4. **Grievance Redressal** — Establishing fair mechanisms for complaint resolution.
5. **Welfare Activities** — Education, healthcare, housing, insurance for workers.
6. **Legislative Influence** — Lobbying for pro-labour laws and policies.
7. **Workers' Participation** — Representation in management decisions and joint consultation bodies.
8. **Social Security** — Pension, gratuity, provident fund benefits.

**Conclusion:**
Trade unions are essential instruments of industrial democracy, ensuring worker voice and fair employment practices.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the various types of strikes with examples.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Strike** is a concerted, collective, and temporary cessation of work by employees as a pressure tactic. Different types include:

1. **Economic Strike** — To secure better wages or working conditions. Example: textile workers' strike for higher pay.
2. **Sympathetic Strike** — In solidarity with workers of another union/industry. Example: transport workers striking in support of striking factory workers.
3. **General Strike** — Industry-wide or nationwide strike. Example: the 2020 Indian farmers' nationwide strike.
4. **Wildcat Strike** — Unofficial, sudden strike without union authorization. Example: spontaneous walkout due to sudden wage cut.
5. **Sit-down Strike** — Workers occupy the workplace but refuse to work. Example: factory workers sitting in the plant premises.
6. **Slowdown (Go-slow)** — Workers deliberately work at reduced pace. Example: assembly line workers reducing output.
7. **Hunger Strike** — Symbolic protest through fasting. Example: Mahatma Gandhi's hunger strikes for labour rights.

**Conclusion:**
Strikes are a powerful but disruptive tool. Legal restrictions under the **Industrial Disputes Act, 1947** govern their conduct.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**What is Grievance Handling? Explain the step-ladder procedure of grievance redressal.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Grievance Handling is the systematic process of receiving, investigating, and resolving employee complaints in a fair, time-bound, and transparent manner.

**Step-Ladder Procedure (Multi-Stage Method):**

**Step 1 — Immediate Supervisor:**
- Employee first approaches their **direct supervisor** within **48 hours** of the grievance arising.
- Supervisor attempts informal resolution within **3 working days**.

**Step 2 — Department Head:**
- If unresolved, the matter escalates to the **Departmental Head / HOD**.
- Written grievance submitted; resolution expected within **7 days**.

**Step 3 — Grievance Committee:**
- A **joint committee** with management and worker representatives investigates the issue.
- Hearing is conducted; recommendation made within **15 days**.

**Step 4 — Top Management:**
- Unresolved cases go to the **CEO / MD / Top Management** for a final decision.
- Decision expected within **30 days**.

**Step 5 — Voluntary Arbitration / Labour Court:**
- Final unresolved cases go to **third-party arbitration** or **Labour Court adjudication** — a binding decision.

**Advantages:**
- Systematic, fair, well-documented, layered escalation.

**Conclusion:**
The step-ladder method balances **speed** with **fairness**, making it the most widely used grievance redressal model.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the importance of Employee Safety and Health at the workplace. Explain the role of Safety Awareness.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of Employee Safety and Health:**

1. **Moral Obligation** — Every employer has a fundamental duty to protect human life.
2. **Legal Compliance** — Mandated by the **Factories Act, 1948** and the **OSH Code, 2020**.
3. **Economic Benefit** — Prevents costly compensation, insurance, and replacement expenses.
4. **Productivity** — Healthy and safe workers are more productive and efficient.
5. **Employee Morale** — Safe workplaces attract and retain talent.
6. **Corporate Reputation** — A safe organization enjoys a strong brand image.

**Role of Safety Awareness:**

1. **Hazard Identification** — Trained workers can spot risks before accidents occur.
2. **Procedure Compliance** — Awareness ensures workers follow SOPs and use PPE correctly.
3. **Emergency Response** — Awareness enables quick, correct response to fire, accidents, chemical spills.
4. **Near-Miss Reporting** — A culture of awareness encourages reporting near-misses, preventing future accidents.
5. **Behavioural Change** — Awareness drives **Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS)**, eliminating unsafe acts.

**Methods to Promote Safety Awareness:**
- Induction and refresher safety training.
- Safety posters, signages, color codes.
- National Safety Week campaigns.
- Toolbox talks and safety drills.
- Rewards for best safety performers.

**Conclusion:**
Safety Awareness transforms safety from a **policy on paper** into a **living culture**, ensuring long-term accident-free operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and lose marks!):**
> 1. **Confusing Strike with Lockout** — Many students interchange them. Remember: **Strike = Workers stop working**; **Lockout = Employer stops workers from working**.
> 2. **Missing the Act/Section references** — Always mention the **Industrial Disputes Act 1947** and the **Trade Unions Act 1926** to earn extra marks.
> 3. **Writing only definitions in 7-mark questions** — Sub-parts in 14-mark questions require **detailed points, examples, and diagrams (if applicable)**. A definition alone fetches only 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring examples** — KTU examiners reward real-world examples. Always include **2-3 examples** in each 7-mark answer.
> 5. **Not mentioning Safety Committees** — When writing about employee safety, always mention the **Safety Committee** requirement under **Section 41C of the Factories Act, 1948**.
> 6. **Confusing collective bargaining with negotiation** — Use precise terms: collective bargaining is a **specific type** of negotiation in industrial relations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 High-Yield Points**

- **Trade Union:** Continuous voluntary association of workers (Trade Unions Act, 1926).
- **Union Objectives:** Wages, conditions, security, welfare, participation, legislation, social security.
- **Central Trade Unions in India:** INTUC, BMS, CITU, AITUC, HMS, UTUC.
- **Collective Bargaining:** Group negotiation between employer and union leading to a **CBA**.
- **Types of Bargaining:** Distributive (win-lose), Integrative (win-win), Productivity, Composite.
- **BATNA:** Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement — gives bargaining power.
- **Strike:** Collective cessation of work by employees (Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, Sec 22-24).
- **Lockout:** Employer's counter-weapon — closing the workplace.
- **Strike Types:** Economic, Sympathetic, General, Wildcat, Sit-down, Go-slow, Hunger, Tool-down.
- **Strike Notice:** 6 weeks in public utility; 14 days in non-public utility.
- **Grievance:** Any real or perceived injustice (Edwin Flippo's definition).
- **Causes:** Wage, work, supervisor, working condition, policy related.
- **Redressal Methods:** Open-Door, Step-Ladder, Grievance Committee, Mediation, Arbitration, Adjudication.
- **Open-Door Policy:** Fastest but least formal.
- **Step-Ladder:** Most widely used — escalates from supervisor to top management.
- **Employee Safety:** Protection from physical hazards.
- **Employee Health:** Protection from diseases, mental stress, ergonomic issues.
- **PPE Types:** Head, Eye, Hearing, Respiratory, Body, Foot, Hand.
- **Hazard Control Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **Safety Committee:** Mandatory in factories with 100+ workers (Section 41C, Factories Act 1948).
- **Heinrich's Domino Theory:** 5-link chain; remove any link to prevent accident.
- **Safety Pyramid:** 1 fatality = 30 lost-time = 300 minor = 3000 near-misses.
- **Safety Awareness Methods:** Training, posters, drills, toolbox talks, BBS, rewards.
- **Key Indian Legislation:** Industrial Disputes Act 1947, Trade Unions Act 1926, Factories Act 1948, OSH Code 2020, ESI Act 1948.
- **Kerala-Specific:** Kerala Shops and Commercial Establishments Act, Kerala Labour Welfare Fund Act.
- **Tripartite System:** Government + Employer + Employee — the foundation of industrial relations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **Definition → Features → Types → Examples → Diagram/Conclusion**. This format consistently scores **12-14/14** marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accident Prevention - Creating Safe Environment - Employee Health Problems and Preventive Measures - Job Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET527 - HUMAN RESOURCES MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accident Prevention - Creating Safe Environment - Employee Health Problems and Preventive Measures - Job Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Accident — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Accident (KTU Board Definition):**
> An *industrial accident* is an unexpected, unplanned, and unwanted event that interrupts the normal workflow of an organization, causes injury (or death) to a person, damage to property, or loss to the organization. It is the failure of a planned sequence of events to occur as intended, and is statistically predictable when its root causes are not addressed.

The **Kerala Factories Rules** and the **Factories Act, 1948** define a "notifiable accident" as one which causes death, or such bodily injury as prevents the worker from working for a period of **48 hours** immediately after the shift in which the accident occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** KTU examiners expect students to differentiate between a *near-miss* (no injury but potential for harm), an *incident* (unwanted event with no injury), and an *accident* (unwanted event resulting in injury or loss). Always use these terms precisely.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Loose Thread" Metaphor

Imagine a freshly knitted woolen sweater. The sweater is the **organization**, the knots are the **safety procedures**, and the worker wearing it is the **employee**. A loose thread is harmless today, but if not trimmed, the entire sweater can unravel with one pull. Similarly, a small safety lapse today becomes tomorrow's catastrophe. Accident prevention is essentially the act of **trimming loose threads** before they snowball into a loss event.

---

## 1.2 Safe Environment — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe Working Environment:**
> A safe working environment is a workplace where all reasonably foreseeable hazards to the physical and psychological well-being of employees have been identified, assessed, controlled, and continuously monitored through a documented **Occupational Health and Safety (OHS)** system.

It comprises three pillars:
- **Physical Safety** — freedom from accidents and physical harm.
- **Psychological Safety** — freedom from harassment, bullying, fear, and undue stress.
- **Ergonomic Safety** — proper fit between the worker and the workstation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "House" Model

Think of a workplace as a house. The **walls** are safety policies, the **roof** is management commitment, the **doors** are training and reporting systems, and the **foundation** is the safety culture. A strong house protects against all weathers — *accidents, diseases, and stress*. A weak roof (no management commitment) means even a small storm (a minor lapse) leaks in and damages the family (employees).

---

## 1.3 Employee Health Problems — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Occupational Health:**
> Occupational health is the promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers in all occupations. It is a multidisciplinary field combining **preventive medicine, ergonomics, industrial hygiene, and HRM**.

The **WHO (World Health Organization)** defines it as: *"The promotion and maintenance of the highest degree of physical, mental and social well-being of workers in all occupations."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Service" Metaphor

Your car needs periodic servicing — oil change, brake check, tyre rotation. Skip the service and the car breaks down mid-road. Employees are like cars. Preventive health check-ups, ergonomically designed seats, and mental wellness sessions are the *periodic servicing* that keeps the workforce running smoothly. Neglect leads to *breakdown* (illness, absenteeism, low productivity).

---

## 1.4 Job Stress — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Job Stress (KTU Board Definition):**
> Job stress is the harmful physical and emotional response that occurs when the demands of the job do not match the capabilities, resources, or needs of the worker. It is a chronic condition, distinct from the everyday *pressure* that energises performance, and is a major antecedent of **burnout**.

$$ \text{Stress} = f(\text{Demand}, \text{Control}, \text{Support}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KYNA – Karasek's Demand-Control Model:** Stress arises when **high psychological demand** is combined with **low decision control**. Add **low social support** and the risk of stress-related illness multiplies.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Metaphor

A pressure cooker is built to handle steam, but if the **whistle (release valve)** is blocked, the pressure builds until the lid explodes. In employees, the whistle is *rest, recreation, autonomy, and support*. Block these release valves (through overwork, no breaks, no autonomy) and the employee "explodes" through anxiety, anger, depression, or physical illness.

---

## 1.5 Key Metrics & Standard Thresholds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Safety Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know:**

| Metric | Formula | Benchmark |
|---|---|---|
| **Accident Frequency Rate (AFR)** | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Accidents} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}}$ | $< 5$ per million man-hours |
| **Accident Severity Rate (ASR)** | $\text{ASR} = \dfrac{\text{Days Lost} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}}$ | $< 50$ per million man-hours |
| **Injury Rate (per 1000 employees)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Injuries}}{\text{Average No. of Employees}} \times 1000$ | Industry specific |
| **Lost Time Injury Frequency Rate (LTIFR)** | $\text{LTIFR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost Time Injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Man-hours Worked}}$ | $< 1$ in safe industries |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CAUSES OF INDUSTRIAL ACCIDENTS — The Domino Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931):** Accidents are the result of a chain of five sequential factors. Remove one domino and the chain is broken.

The five dominos are:
1. **Ancestry and Social Environment** — hereditary traits, upbringing.
2. **Personal Fault** — recklessness, carelessness, ill-health.
3. **Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition** — the immediate trigger.
4. **The Accident** — the visible event.
5. **Injury or Damage** — the final consequence.

Modern OHS (Occupational Health and Safety) has refined this into the **Bird & Germain Loss Causation Model**, which adds **lack of controls** as the root cause.

---

## 2.2 THE ACCIDENT PREVENTION FRAMEWORK — 4 E's and 4 P's

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 E's of Accident Prevention (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **E1 — Engineering Control:** Modify machinery, tools, layout.
> - **E2 — Education:** Training, awareness, induction programs.
> - **E3 — Enforcement:** Compliance with safety rules and legal statutes.
> - **E4 — Environment Control:** Adequate lighting, ventilation, noise reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 P's of Safety Culture:**
> - **Policy** — written safety policy at the top.
> - **Procedure** — SOPs for every risky task.
> - **Practice** — regular drills, mock evacuations.
> - **Performance** — measurable safety KPIs.

---

## 2.3 PRINCIPLES OF ACCIDENT PREVENTION (Petersen's 7 Principles)

1. **P1 — Get Top Management Commitment** — safety is a line function, not a staff function.
2. **P2 — Accept the Economic Value of Safety** — the cost of prevention is far lower than the cost of an accident.
3. **P3 — Maintain a Genuine Concern for People** — treat employees as assets, not costs.
4. **P4 — Provide Safe Equipment and Materials** — ergonomic design and quality tools.
5. **P5 — Utilise Proper Selection, Placement, and Training** — fit the man to the job.
6. **P6 — Hold Everyone Accountable** — zero-tolerance to violations.
7. **P7 — Establish an Effective Safety Organisation** — safety officer, safety committee, audits.

---

## 2.4 CREATING A SAFE WORKING ENVIRONMENT — The 7-Point Model

| S.No | Element | Practical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical Layout** | Adequate space, anti-slip flooring, clear walkways |
| 2 | **Lighting & Ventilation** | Min **300 lux** for general work; **500 lux** for precision work |
| 3 | **Noise Control** | $< 85$ dB (A) exposure limit for 8-hour shift |
| 4 | **Temperature & Humidity** | $20°\text{C} – 25°\text{C}$ optimum; humidity $40$–$60\%$ |
| 5 | **Fire Safety** | Fire extinguishers every $15$ metres; exit doors marked |
| 6 | **First Aid** | One first-aid box per $150$ workers; one trained first-aider per shift |
| 7 | **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** | Hard hats, gloves, goggles, earplugs — provided free of cost |

---

## 2.5 OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH HAZARDS — The 5 Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Occupational Hazards (KTU Syllabus Mandate):**

1. **Physical Hazards** — noise, heat, radiation, vibration, electric shock.
2. **Chemical Hazards** — dust, fumes, gases, acids, solvents.
3. **Biological Hazards** — viruses, bacteria, fungi (e.g., healthcare, food industry).
4. **Ergonomic Hazards** — repetitive motion, awkward posture, heavy lifting.
5. **Psychosocial Hazards** — stress, harassment, violence, isolation.

### Common Occupational Diseases

| Hazard | Disease |
|---|---|
| Silica dust | **Silicosis** (lung fibrosis) |
| Asbestos | **Asbestosis / Mesothelioma** |
| Coal dust | **Pneumoconiosis** (Black Lung) |
| Lead exposure | **Plumbism** (lead poisoning) |
| Benzene | **Leukemia** |
| Repetitive motion | **Carpal Tunnel Syndrome** |
| Continuous noise | **Occupational Hearing Loss** |

---

## 2.6 THEORIES OF JOB STRESS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Theoretical Pillars of Job Stress (Board Hot Topic):**

### A. Karasek's Demand-Control Model (1979)
- **High Demand + Low Control = High Strain Job** (e.g., assembly line worker).
- **High Demand + High Control = Active Job** (e.g., surgeon, executive).
- **Low Demand + Low Control = Passive Job** (e.g., watchman).
- **Low Demand + High Control = Low Strain Job** (e.g., gardener).

### B. Siegrist's Effort-Reward Imbalance Model (1996)
$$ \frac{\text{Effort}}{\text{Reward}} > 1 \implies \text{Stress} $$

If the **extrinsic effort** (workload, hours) exceeds the **extrinsic reward** (pay, recognition) combined with **intrinsic overcommitment** (workaholism), the worker experiences chronic stress.

### C. Maslach's Burnout Model
Burnout is the end-stage of chronic job stress and has three dimensions:
- **Emotional Exhaustion** — feeling drained.
- **Depersonalization (Cynicism)** — distancing from work.
- **Reduced Personal Accomplishment** — feeling ineffective.

---

## 2.7 SOURCES OF JOB STRESS

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Organizational** | Role ambiguity, role conflict, role overload, poor communication |
| **Work Environment** | Noise, heat, overcrowding, lack of privacy |
| **Work-Life Balance** | Shift work, long hours, on-call duties |
| **Interpersonal** | Conflicts, harassment, bullying, discrimination |
| **Career-Related** | Job insecurity, lack of growth, obsolescence |
| **Task-Related** | Repetitive work, lack of variety, low autonomy |

---

## 2.8 EFFECTS OF JOB STRESS

$$ \text{Stress Effects} = \begin{cases} \text{Physical: Hypertension, ulcers, headaches, insomnia, CAD} \\ \text{Psychological: Anxiety, depression, irritability, low morale} \\ \text{Behavioral: Absenteeism, turnover, substance abuse, accidents} \end{cases} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Stress is **NOT** a weakness. It is a *response* to an imbalanced demand-resource equation. Do not write in exams that "stress is the employee's fault."

---

## 2.9 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Accident Frequency Rate | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{H}$ | $N$ = no. of accidents, $H$ = man-hours |
| Accident Severity Rate | $\text{ASR} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{H}$ | $D$ = days lost |
| INCIDENT RATE | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{Injuries}}{\text{Employees}} \times 200000$ | OSHA standard |
| Demand-Reward Ratio | $\text{DRR} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | $> 1$ indicates stress |
| Absenteeism Rate | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{\text{Days Lost}}{\text{Total Working Days}} \times 100$ | $> 3\%$ is concerning |
| Stress-Productivity Inverse Law | $P = P_0 - k \cdot S$ | Productivity drops linearly with stress above threshold |
| Heinrich's Ratio | $1 : 29 : 300$ | 1 major injury : 29 minor : 300 near-misses |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION: HENRICH'S SAFETY RATIO

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently asks students to compute safety ratios using a numerical scenario. Heinrich's 1:29:300 ratio (also called the *Safety Triangle*) is the foundation.

### The Underlying Logic

Heinrich's empirical study of **50,000 accident cases** revealed a constant statistical relationship:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For every } &1 \text{ major injury (fatal/disabling), \\
\text{there are } &29 \text{ minor injuries requiring first aid only, \\
\text{and } &300 \text{ near-miss incidents with no injury at all.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Application — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A factory has recorded **870 near-misses** in 2023. Calculate the expected number of (a) minor injuries and (b) major injuries, using Heinrich's Triangle.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data:**
$$
N_{\text{near-miss}} = 870
$$

**Step 2 — Recall Heinrich's ratio:**
$$
\text{Major : Minor : Near-Miss} = 1 : 29 : 300
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the common multiplier (k):**
$$
k = \dfrac{N_{\text{near-miss}}}{300} = \dfrac{870}{300} = 2.9
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expected minor injuries:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{minor}} &= k \times 29 \\
&= 2.9 \times 29 \\
&= 84.1 \approx 84 \text{ minor injuries}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the expected major injuries:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{major}} &= k \times 1 \\
&= 2.9 \times 1 \\
&= 2.9 \approx 3 \text{ major injuries}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation for the HR Manager:**
The presence of **870 near-misses** is a leading indicator that **3 major accidents** are likely in the absence of intervention. This is the *power* of the safety triangle: it converts invisible near-miss data into a forecast of major losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always state the *practical implication* in the last step. KTU examiners reward application-level thinking.

---

## 3.2 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION: ACCIDENT FREQUENCY RATE (AFR) — KTU NUMERICAL

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit employs **500 workers**. Each worker works **8 hours/day** for **300 days/year**. During the year, **25 reportable accidents** occurred, resulting in a total of **350 days lost**. Calculate the **Accident Frequency Rate** and **Accident Severity Rate**.

**Step 1 — Compute total man-hours worked:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \text{Workers} \times \text{Hours/Day} \times \text{Days/Year} \\
H &= 500 \times 8 \times 300 \\
H &= 1,200,000 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the AFR formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AFR} &= \dfrac{N \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \dfrac{25 \times 10^6}{1,200,000} \\
&= \dfrac{25,000,000}{1,200,000} \\
&= 20.83 \text{ accidents per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ASR formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASR} &= \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{H} \\
&= \dfrac{350 \times 10^6}{1,200,000} \\
&= \dfrac{350,000,000}{1,200,000} \\
&= 291.67 \text{ days lost per million man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**
- An AFR of **20.83** is **significantly above** the safe benchmark of $< 5$, indicating a **high-frequency, unsafe workplace**.
- An ASR of **291.67** is **far above** the safe benchmark of $< 50$, indicating that when accidents occur, they are **severe**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students often forget to multiply by $10^6$. Always remember that AFR/ASR are expressed **per million man-hours**, not absolute numbers.

---

## 3.3 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION: SIEGRIST'S EFFORT-REWARD IMBALANCE

**Problem:** An employee works **60 hours/week** (effort score = 80 on a 100-point scale) but earns a basic salary with no bonus or recognition (reward score = 50). His intrinsic overcommitment score is **40**. Calculate the Effort-Reward Ratio and comment on his stress level.

**Step 1 — Recall the model:**
$$
\text{ERI} = \dfrac{\text{Effort}}{(\text{Reward} \times \text{Overcommitment Factor})}
$$

**Step 2 — Adjust the reward for overcommitment:**
A person with high overcommitment *under-values* the reward. Siegrist's correction:
$$
R_{\text{adjusted}} = R + (\text{OC} \times 0.5) = 50 + (40 \times 0.5) = 70
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ERI} &= \dfrac{80}{70} \\
&= 1.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**
Since $\text{ERI} > 1$, the employee is in a state of **effort-reward imbalance** and is at high risk of **psychosomatic illness, cardiovascular disease, and depression** within 2-3 years if uncorrected.

---

## 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — STRESS MANAGEMENT INTERVENTIONS

| Level | Intervention | Examples | Time-Frame |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Individual** | Personal coping | Time management, meditation, exercise, EAP counselling | Short-term |
| **Group/Team** | Team building | Conflict resolution, peer support groups | Medium-term |
| **Organizational** | Job redesign | Job enrichment, role clarification, autonomy, flexible hours | Long-term |
| **Strategic** | Culture change | Wellness programs, leadership training, open-door policies | Permanent |

---

## 3.5 KTU-STYLE CASE WALK-THROUGH: ACCIDENT INVESTIGATION

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5-Whys Technique (Toyota Production System) — used in root-cause analysis.**

A worker slipped on oil near Machine 7.

- **Why 1:** He slipped on oil. → **Why 2:** Oil was spilled. → **Why 3:** A fitting was loose. → **Why 4:** It was not replaced during maintenance. → **Why 5:** There was no preventive maintenance schedule.

**Root cause:** Systemic failure in preventive maintenance — not the worker's carelessness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Insight:** KTU examiners reward students who identify **systemic** causes over **individual** blame. This aligns with the modern "Just Culture" framework.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ACCIDENT CAUSATION — BLOCK-LEVEL FUNCTIONAL ARCHITECTURE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROOT CAUSES] --> B[Personal Factors]
    A --> C[Job Factors]
    A --> D[Organisational Factors]
    A --> E[Environmental Factors]
    B --> F[Unsafe Acts]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Unsafe Conditions]
    E --> G
    F --> H[ACCIDENT EVENT]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Injury Severity}
    I --> J[First Aid Case]
    I --> K[Medical Case]
    I --> L[Lost Time Injury]
    I --> M[Fatality]
    H --> N[Near Miss - No Injury]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that the **root causes** branch into four sources, which in turn produce either **unsafe acts** or **unsafe conditions**, both of which converge at the accident event. The outcome is then classified by severity. Near-misses sit *parallel* to injuries — they are early warning signals.

---

## 4.2 SAFE WORKPLACE CREATION — INPUT-PROCESS-OUTCOME MODEL

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Management Commitment] --> PROC1[Safety Policy]
    INPUT2[Employee Involvement] --> PROC1
    INPUT3[Hazard Identification] --> PROC2[Risk Assessment]
    INPUT4[Legal Compliance] --> PROC2
    PROC1 --> OUT1[Documented Procedures]
    PROC2 --> OUT2[Control Measures]
    PROC1 --> OUT3[Training Programs]
    PROC2 --> OUT3
    OUT1 --> GOAL[ZERO ACCIDENT WORKPLACE]
    OUT2 --> GOAL
    OUT3 --> GOAL
```

---

## 4.3 JOB STRESS MODEL — KARASEK FRAMEWORK

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DEMAND[PSYCHOLOGICAL DEMAND] -->|+| STRAIN1[High Strain Job]
    DEMAND -->|-| STRAIN2[Passive Job]
    DEMAND -->|+| STRAIN3[Active Job]
    DEMAND -->|-| STRAIN4[Low Strain Job]
    CONTROL[DECISION CONTROL] -->|-| STRAIN1
    CONTROL -->|-| STRAIN2
    CONTROL -->|+| STRAIN3
    CONTROL -->|+| STRAIN4
    STRAIN1 --> OUT1[Stress, Burnout, CVD]
    STRAIN3 --> OUT2[Growth, Learning]
    STRAIN2 --> OUT3[Loss of Skills]
    STRAIN4 --> OUT4[Comfort, Low Motivation]
```

---

## 4.4 STRESS MANAGEMENT FLOWCHART

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STRESSOR IDENTIFIED] --> B{Severity Check}
    B -->|Mild| C[Individual Coping - Time Mgmt, Exercise]
    B -->|Moderate| D[Group Support - Counselling, EAP]
    B -->|Severe| E[Organisational Intervention - Job Redesign]
    C --> F[Monitor Progress Monthly]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Policy Reform - Flexible Hours, Wellness Program]
    G --> F
    F --> H{Stress Reduced?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Document Best Practice]
    H -->|No| B
```

---

## 4.5 OCCUPATIONAL HEALTH — HAZARD-CONTROL MATRIX

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HAZ[Hazard Source] -->|Eliminate| CTRL1[Elimination - Best]
    HAZ -->|Substitute| CTRL2[Substitution]
    HAZ -->|Engineering| CTRL3[Guards, Ventilation, Fencing]
    HAZ -->|Administrative| CTRL4[Rotation, Training, SOPs]
    HAZ -->|PPE| CTRL5[Last Resort - Helmets, Gloves, Goggles]
    CTRL1 --> SAFE[SAFE WORKER]
    CTRL2 --> SAFE
    CTRL3 --> SAFE
    CTRL4 --> SAFE
    CTRL5 --> SAFE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (KTU Board Favourite):** The diagram follows the **NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls**. Always recommend the *highest level* (Elimination) before resorting to PPE.

---

## 4.6 INTEGRATED SAFETY & WELLNESS ECOSYSTEM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POLICY[TOP MANAGEMENT - Safety Policy] --> SYS[Safety Committee + Safety Officer]
    SYS --> TRAIN[Induction + On-job Training]
    SYS --> AUDIT[Periodic Audits and Inspections]
    SYS --> REPORT[Incident Reporting System]
    TRAIN --> EMP[Employees - Aware and Skilled]
    AUDIT --> EMP
    REPORT --> EMP
    EMP --> HEALTH[Employee Health Programs]
    EMP --> STRESS[Stress Management Programs]
    HEALTH --> PROD[Higher Productivity]
    STRESS --> PROD
    PROD --> LOOP[Feedback to TOP MANAGEMENT]
    LOOP --> POLICY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The Continuous Improvement Loop:** Note how feedback from the bottom flows back to the top, embodying the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle of Deming. This is the *gold standard* answer in KTU 14-mark questions on safety management.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define the term "industrial accident" and list Heinrich's five dominos.**

**Model Answer:**

> An industrial accident is an unexpected, unplanned event causing injury, loss, or damage, interrupting normal work. Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) states accidents are caused by a chain of five factors, in sequence:
>
> 1. Ancestry and Social Environment
> 2. Personal Fault (carelessness)
> 3. Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition
> 4. The Accident
> 5. Injury or Damage
>
> Removing any one domino breaks the chain. This model emphasises that **personal fault is only one factor**, and management has the responsibility to remove unsafe conditions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between "eustress" and "distress".**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Eustress (Positive Stress) | Distress (Negative Stress) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Stress that motivates and improves performance | Stress that harms health and productivity |
| **Duration** | Short-term, acute | Long-term, chronic |
| **Effect** | Energising, focused | Exhausting, anxious |
| **Example** | Pre-exam revision rush | Continuous work overload without rest |
| **Outcome** | Growth and achievement | Burnout and illness |

> Hans Selye (the father of stress research) coined these terms in 1974.

---

## 5.2 PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): ACCIDENT PREVENTION & SAFE ENVIRONMENT**
**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **causes of industrial accidents** with reference to Heinrich's Domino Theory and the **4 E's of accident prevention**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **steps an HR department must take to create a safe working environment**, citing at least five concrete measures and the relevant statutory provisions under the **Factories Act, 1948**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Introduction:** Industrial accidents are preventable events that arise from a chain of controllable causes. Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931) is the classical framework.

**2. Heinrich's Five Dominos — Sequential Analysis:**
- **Domino 1 — Ancestry and Social Environment:** Hereditary traits, upbringing, peer influence (e.g., a worker from a reckless family may inherit risk-taking behaviour).
- **Domino 2 — Personal Fault:** Carelessness, lack of skill, ill-health, fatigue, alcohol use.
- **Domino 3 — Unsafe Act or Unsafe Condition:** Operating machinery without guards, no PPE, poor lighting, slippery floor.
- **Domino 4 — The Accident:** The actual contact with a hazardous substance or energy source.
- **Domino 5 — Injury or Damage:** The final consequence — physical harm or property loss.

**3. The 4 E's of Accident Prevention:**
- **Engineering Control:** Machine guarding, fencing, automatic cut-offs, ergonomically designed tools.
- **Education:** Induction training, refresher courses, safety posters, mock drills.
- **Enforcement:** Disciplinary action for violations, statutory compliance audits, safety officers with authority.
- **Environment Control:** Proper lighting (min 300 lux), ventilation, noise control (<85 dB), housekeeping.

**4. Conclusion:** Modern OHS has moved beyond individual blame to a **systems approach** — Heinrich's dominos today are replaced by the **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990), where multiple layers of defence must align for an accident to occur.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**1. Introduction:** The Factories Act, 1948 (Sections 6–8, 11, 21, 38) places a legal duty on the occupier to ensure the health, safety, and welfare of all workers.

**2. Five Concrete HR-Driven Measures:**

| Measure | Statutory Reference | Practical Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Safety Policy & Committee** | Section 11 | Written policy; Safety Committee with equal worker-management representation |
| **Safety Officer** | Section 40B | Appoint a qualified Safety Officer where 1000+ workers are employed |
| **Maintenance of Plant & Machinery** | Section 21 | Annual fitness certification, preventive maintenance schedule |
| **Safety Training** | Section 11B | Induction training within 7 days of joining, on-the-job safety training |
| **Reporting of Accidents** | Section 88 | Notify the Inspector within 24 hours of any notifiable accident |
| **Welfare Provisions** | Sections 42–48 | Drinking water, first aid, rest rooms, lighting, ventilation |

**3. Additional Modern Measures:**
- Behaviour-Based Safety (BBS) programs.
- Near-miss reporting systems.
- Wellness and ergonomics programs.

**4. Conclusion:** A safe environment is not merely legal compliance; it is a **strategic HRM function** that drives productivity, reduces compensation costs, and builds employer brand.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): EMPLOYEE HEALTH & JOB STRESS**
**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Identify the **major occupational health hazards** and explain the **preventive measures** HR can implement under each category.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Analyse the **sources and effects of job stress** using **Karasek's Demand-Control Model**, and recommend a comprehensive **stress management program** for a software company with 500 employees.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**1. Introduction:** The WHO defines occupational health as the promotion of the highest degree of physical, mental, and social well-being of workers.

**2. Five Major Categories of Occupational Hazards and HR Preventive Measures:**

**i. Physical Hazards** — noise, heat, radiation, vibration.
- *Preventive HR measure:* Provide PPE, enforce shift rotation, conduct audiometric tests every 6 months, install acoustic enclosures.

**ii. Chemical Hazards** — dust, fumes, acids, solvents.
- *Preventive HR measure:* Substitute harmful chemicals (e.g., benzene → toluene), provide respirators, conduct regular air-quality monitoring, train in Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS).

**iii. Biological Hazards** — viruses, bacteria (relevant in healthcare, food processing).
- *Preventive HR measure:* Mandatory vaccination, hygiene training, periodic health check-ups, isolation protocols.

**iv. Ergonomic Hazards** — repetitive motion, awkward posture, heavy lifting.
- *Preventive HR measure:* Ergonomic workstation design, job rotation, micro-breaks every 90 minutes, lifting aids.

**v. Psychosocial Hazards** — stress, harassment, isolation.
- *Preventive HR measure:* Employee Assistance Programs (EAP), grievance redressal committee, anti-harassment policy (POSH Act, 2013), flexible work arrangements.

**3. Conclusion:** HR's role has evolved from a *reactive* medical officer function to a *proactive* wellness architect, integrating the **NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls** into daily operations.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**1. Introduction:** Job stress is the harmful response when job demands exceed the worker's resources. Karasek's model (1979) is the most cited framework.

**2. Sources of Job Stress in a Software Company:**
- Tight project deadlines and unrealistic sprint commitments.
- On-call duties and global client time-zones.
- Long screen hours causing physical and mental fatigue.
- Role ambiguity in cross-functional Agile teams.
- Fear of layoffs in a competitive IT market.

**3. Effects of Job Stress:**
- **Physical:** Insomnia, eye strain, hypertension, IBS.
- **Psychological:** Anxiety, depression, reduced creativity.
- **Behavioural:** Absenteeism, attrition, substance abuse, *bug-induced* errors.

**4. Applying Karasek's Model to Software Jobs:**
Most software developers fall in the **High Demand + Low Control** quadrant (e.g., junior developers following fixed specifications). This is the **High-Strain Job** category — highest risk of burnout and cardiovascular disease within 3-5 years.

**5. Recommended Stress Management Program:**

| Level | Initiative |
|---|---|
| **Individual** | Free yoga/meditation classes, ergonomic chair subsidy, Eye-care allowance, EAP counselling (1-on-1) |
| **Team** | Sprint retrospectives with a "wellness" agenda, no-meeting Fridays, peer-buddy system |
| **Organisational** | Job redesign to give developers more autonomy (Scrum Master's role), flexible hours, "right to disconnect" policy, skill-building opportunities |
| **Strategic** | Annual Employee Engagement Survey, leadership coaching, chronic-stress KPI in HR dashboard |

**6. Conclusion:** A holistic stress management program reduces attrition by 18-25% (per SHRM data) and increases software quality. Karasek's model is a useful diagnostic tool, but intervention must be **systemic, not individualistic**.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "accident" with "incident":** An *accident* causes injury; an *incident* may not. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Writing "stress is the employee's fault":** This is the *most common* answer error. Stress is **systemic**, not personal. The model is demand-resource imbalance.
> 3. **Ignoring legal provisions:** Any 14-mark question on safety/accidents **must cite the Factories Act, 1948** or the **POSH Act, 2013**. Generic answers without statute lose 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up formulas:** $\text{AFR}$ uses *number of accidents*; $\text{ASR}$ uses *days lost*. Many students invert these.
> 5. **Skipping the "Conclusion" in long answers:** KTU evaluators explicitly reward a 2-line conclusion tying the answer to HRM practice.
> 6. **Listing without classifying:** In occupational hazards, *always* classify into the 5 categories (Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial). A flat list loses marks.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Summary:**

- **Accident Prevention** is built on **Petersen's 7 Principles** and the **4 E's (Engineering, Education, Enforcement, Environment)**.
- The **Safety Triangle (Heinrich)** is **1 : 29 : 300** (Major : Minor : Near-miss). Near-misses are the *leading indicator* of major accidents.
- The **Factories Act, 1948** mandates a Safety Officer (Sec 40B), Safety Committee (Sec 11), and accident reporting within 24 hours (Sec 88).
- A **Safe Environment** has three pillars: **Physical, Psychological, Ergonomic**.
- **Occupational Hazards** are five types: **P-C-B-E-P** (Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial). Use the **NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls**: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- Common occupational diseases: **Silicosis, Asbestosis, Pneumoconiosis, Plumbism, Carpal Tunnel Syndrome**.
- **Job Stress** is **demand-resource imbalance** (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984). It is **not** a personal weakness.
- **Karasek's Model:** **High Demand + Low Control = High Strain** (most dangerous).
- **Siegrist's Model:** $\dfrac{\text{Effort}}{\text{Reward}} > 1 \implies$ stress.
- **Maslach's Burnout** has 3 dimensions: **Emotional Exhaustion, Depersonalization, Reduced Accomplishment**.
- **Stress Management** operates at **4 levels**: Individual, Group, Organizational, Strategic.
- Standard safety metrics: **AFR, ASR, LTIFR, OSHA Incident Rate**. Always multiply by $10^6$ or $200,000$ — **never forget the constant**.
- **Selye's GAS** (General Adaptation Syndrome): **Alarm → Resistance → Exhaustion**. Chronic stress = Exhaustion stage = Burnout.
- **Bird & Germain's Loss Causation Model** is the modern upgrade of Heinrich's Domino Theory.
- **Just Culture** framework: balance between *no-blame* (systemic) and *accountability* (reckless behaviour).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Final Word:** This module tests both **conceptual clarity** (Part A) and **applied HR thinking** (Part B). Memorise formulas, theories, and the Factories Act sections — they appear every KTU exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Employee Assistance Programmes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET527 - HUMAN RESOURCES MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Employee Assistance Programmes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Standardised)**
> **Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)** is a confidential, employer-sponsored, structured workplace intervention programme designed to help employees identify, resolve, and manage personal, work-related, health, or behavioural problems that adversely affect their job performance, well-being, and overall organisational productivity.

According to the **International Employee Assistance Professionals Association (EAPAA)**, an EAP is a *"workplace-based programme designed to assist in the identification and resolution of productivity problems associated with employees impaired by personal concerns including, but not limited to, health, marital, family, financial, alcohol, drug, legal, emotional, or other personal concerns which may adversely affect employee job performance."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Board-Exam Ready Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **EAP** | Employee Assistance Programme |
| **Confidentiality** | Strict privacy of employee records; only aggregate data shared with employer |
| **Referral Path** | Route through which an employee enters the EAP (self, supervisor, union, family) |
| **Utilisation Rate** | Percentage of employees actually using the EAP services in a year |
| **Counsellor / Clinician** | Trained professional (often external) who delivers EAP services |
| **Core Technology** | The scientifically validated methods used by EAPs (assessment, counselling, referral) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Hospital"

> [!NOTE] **The "First-Aid Kit of Corporate Wellness" Analogy**
> Imagine your workplace as a long-distance marathon. While most runners (employees) can keep pace on their own, some may develop blisters, dehydration, or emotional exhaustion mid-way. An **EAP is the medical support tent on the marathon route** — a confidential, voluntary, and free aid station where any runner can walk in, get a quick diagnosis, receive first-aid (counselling), or be referred to a specialist hospital (rehab, psychiatrist, financial advisor) if the problem is serious.
>
> - The runner is the **employee**.
> - The marathon is the **work-life journey**.
> - The medical tent is the **EAP**.
> - The first-aid staff are the **EAP counsellors**.
> - The hospital referral is the **external specialist service**.
>
> The runner does not have to be "officially sick" to use the tent — prevention, early diagnosis, and quick recovery are the goals.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution (Background)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Origin Timeline (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)**
> - **1940s**: EAPs originated in the United States as **Occupational Alcoholism Programmes (OAPs)**, focusing narrowly on alcoholic employees.
> - **1970s**: Expanded to include drug abuse, hence the term **Employee Assistance Programme** replaced "alcoholism programme."
> - **1980s**: Broadened further to include mental health, family issues, financial counselling, legal aid — the **"Broad Brush" Model** emerged.
> - **1990s onwards**: Introduction of **Managed Care** and **Integrated Models** combining internal + external EAPs.
> - **2000s – Present**: Digital EAPs, **E-Counselling**, **Wellness Portals**, and 24×7 helplines dominate.
> - **In India**: Pioneered by large IT/ITeS firms (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL) in the 1990s; now adopted by BPOs, banks, and PSUs.

## 1.5 Scope of EAP (What Problems Does It Cover?)

> [!NOTE] **The "Big 8" Problem Categories Addressed by EAPs**
> 1. **Alcohol & Substance Abuse** (legacy origin)
> 2. **Mental Health** (stress, anxiety, depression, burnout)
> 3. **Marital & Family Issues** (divorce, parenting, elder care)
> 4. **Financial Problems** (debt, bankruptcy, tax issues)
> 5. **Legal Concerns** (consumer disputes, criminal cases, family law)
> 6. **Workplace Conflicts** (harassment, bullying, interpersonal disputes)
> 7. **Health & Lifestyle** (chronic illness, smoking cessation, weight management)
> 8. **Crisis Intervention** (grief, trauma, suicidal ideation, disaster response)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of Employee Assistance Programmes

The objectives of an EAP operate on **three levels** — the employee level, the employer level, and the social level. This is a high-frequency 7-mark KTU question.

### A. Objectives from the **Employee's Perspective**
- Early identification and resolution of personal problems before they become critical.
- Free, confidential, and professional counselling support.
- Improved work-life balance and overall quality of life.
- Protection from job loss due to treatable personal issues.
- Access to specialist resources that the employee may not otherwise afford.

### B. Objectives from the **Employer's Perspective**
- Improved **productivity** and **job performance**.
- Reduction in **absenteeism**, **presenteeism**, and **tardiness**.
- Lower **employee turnover** and associated replacement costs (often **1.5× to 2× annual salary**).
- Reduced **medical / insurance** costs (especially for stress-related claims).
- Better **workplace morale**, employee engagement, and employer branding.
- Lower incidence of workplace accidents and disciplinary issues.
- Compliance with **legal and statutory** obligations (e.g., POSH Act 2013, mental-health provisions of Factories Act).

### C. Objectives from the **Societal / Community Perspective**
- Promoting **corporate social responsibility (CSR)**.
- Reducing family disintegration, substance abuse, and public health burden.
- Building a healthier, more stable workforce for the national economy.

## 2.2 Guiding Principles (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 7 Cardinal Principles of an Effective EAP**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — The bedrock principle. No information disclosed without written consent.
> 2. **Voluntariness** — Employees use the programme by choice; no coercion.
> 3. **Accessibility** — Easy to access (24×7 helpline, multilingual, onsite/offsite).
> 4. **Professionalism** — Delivered by qualified, licensed counsellors.
> 5. **Neutrality** — The EAP is independent of management's disciplinary arm.
> 6. **Anonymity (where possible)** — Aggregate, de-identified data only to employer.
> 7. **Continuity** — Long-term commitment, not a one-time intervention.

## 2.3 Types / Models of EAPs (Critical for 14-Mark Questions)

### Model 1: **Internal EAP**
The EAP is staffed and managed entirely **inside** the organisation by in-house counsellors.
- **Pros**: Familiar with culture; onsite accessibility; lower long-term cost.
- **Cons**: Fear of breach of confidentiality; stigma; limited specialisation.
- **Best for**: Very large organisations (e.g., Infosys, TCS, Indian Railways).

### Model 2: **External EAP**
Services are outsourced to a **third-party vendor** (e.g., Optum, MediAssist-Wellness, Vantage Healthcare, 1to1help.net in India).
- **Pros**: High confidentiality; 24×7 access; specialised expertise; cost-efficient for SMEs.
- **Cons**: Less cultural fit; requires strong Service Level Agreement (SLA).
- **Best for**: SMEs, BPOs, geographically dispersed firms.

### Model 3: **Blended / Hybrid EAP**
A combination of internal and external services. Internal counsellor handles initial screening; complex cases are referred externally.
- **Pros**: Best of both worlds.
- **Cons**: Coordination challenges; need clear protocols.

### Model 4: **Consortium EAP**
Multiple small firms in an industrial cluster **pool resources** to share a common EAP.
- **Pros**: Lowest per-employee cost; good for clusters like Tirupur, Ludhiana, Surat.
- **Cons**: Slower response time; cross-employer confidentiality concerns.

### Model 5: **Peer-Based / Member-Assistance Programme (MAP)**
Trained **peer volunteers** from the workforce serve as first responders before referral to professional help.
- **Pros**: Cultural sensitivity; high trust.
- **Cons**: Limited expertise; risk of boundary violations.

## 2.4 Components / Features of an EAP (Architecture)

> [!NOTE] **Standard 6-Component EAP Architecture**
> 1. **Identification & Awareness** — Posters, orientations, manager training, intranet portals.
> 2. **Assessment** — Initial screening by counsellor to understand the issue's nature and severity.
> 3. **Counselling** — Short-term solution-focused therapy (typically **1 to 8 sessions**).
> 4. **Referral & Follow-up** — Linking to external specialists (psychiatrist, debt counsellor, lawyer).
> 5. **Crisis Intervention** — 24×7 hotline, suicide-ideation protocols, trauma debriefing.
> 6. **Evaluation & Feedback** — Quarterly utilisation reports, employee satisfaction surveys, ROI measurement.

## 2.5 Benefits of EAP (HRM Exam Tabular Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT] **The "Quadruple Bottom Line" of EAP Benefits**
> | Stakeholder | Key Benefits |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | **Employees** | Confidential help, better well-being, job security, work-life balance |
> | **Employer** | Higher productivity, lower absenteeism, lower turnover, lower healthcare cost, legal compliance |
> | **HR Department** | Better industrial relations, reduced grievance handling, stronger employer brand |
> | **Society** | Lower substance abuse, stronger families, healthy workforce, CSR compliance |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **EAP Utilisation Rate** | $\text{Utilisation \%} = \dfrac{\text{Number of unique EAP users}}{\text{Total eligible employees}} \times 100$ | Typically **3 % to 8 %** annually in healthy EAPs |
| **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Savings from reduced absenteeism + turnover + healthcare}) - (\text{EAP cost})}{\text{EAP cost}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark: **\$3 to \$6 saved per \$1 invested** |
| **Absenteeism Reduction** | $\Delta A = A_{\text{before}} - A_{\text{after}}$ (in days per employee per year) | Typical reduction: **0.5 to 2.5 days/employee/year** |
| **Turnover Cost (Replacement)** | $C_{\text{replacement}} \approx 1.5 \times \text{Annual Salary} \text{ to } 2.0 \times \text{Annual Salary}$ | Per separated employee |
| **Cost per Employee per Year (PEPY)** | $\text{PEPY} = \dfrac{\text{Total annual EAP cost}}{\text{Number of covered employees}}$ | India range: **₹300 – ₹1500 PEPY** |
| **Confidentiality Breach Risk** | Should remain **Zero** (legally and ethically) | — |
| **Counselling Sessions Cap** | Generally **1 to 8 short-term sessions per issue** | Crisis: unlimited |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

> [!NOTE] **Why HR / Engineering Managers Study EAP**
> In high-stress industries such as **IT services, BPO/KPO, construction, nuclear power, oil & gas, aviation, and manufacturing**, EAPs are no longer optional — they are integrated into **occupational health & safety (OHS)** and **ISO 45001** frameworks. For KTU engineering graduates who will become managers/team leaders, understanding EAPs is essential for:
> - Building psychologically safe teams.
> - Recognising early signs of burnout in project deadlines.
> - Complying with the **Mental Healthcare Act 2017** and the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016**.
> - Reducing costly engineering errors caused by human-factor stress.

# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Process & Analytical Breakdown

## 3.1 The Standard EAP Life-Cycle (10 Steps)

This is the most exam-relevant sequence. KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the step-by-step process of implementing an EAP"* (7 or 14 marks).

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 10-Step EAP Implementation Roadmap**
> 1. **Need Assessment** — Surveys, focus groups, exit interviews, absenteeism data analysis.
> 2. **Top Management Commitment** — Budget approval, written policy, CEO endorsement.
> 3. **Policy Formulation** — Drafting the EAP policy document (objectives, scope, confidentiality clause, eligibility).
> 4. **Vendor Selection (if external)** — RFP, evaluation, SLA negotiation, pilot.
> 5. **Designing the Service Package** — Counselling hours, helpline, e-counselling, workshops, manager training.
> 6. **Communication & Awareness** — Launch campaigns, posters, mailers, induction modules, intranet.
> 7. **Training of Managers & Peer Volunteers** — Identification, referral, de-stigmatisation training.
> 8. **Service Delivery Begins** — Intake, assessment, counselling, referral, follow-up.
> 9. **Monitoring & MIS Reports** — Quarterly aggregated utilisation reports (no individual data).
> 10. **Evaluation & Continuous Improvement** — Annual review, ROI calculation, employee feedback.

## 3.2 Analytical Breakdown of the EAP Counselling Process

When an employee enters the EAP, the counsellor follows a structured 5-stage clinical pathway:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Stage 1: Intake} &\rightarrow \text{Employee contacts the EAP (call, walk-in, online, referred).} \\
\textbf{Stage 2: Assessment} &\rightarrow \text{Counsellor evaluates problem type, severity, risk level.} \\
\textbf{Stage 3: Short-term Counselling} &\rightarrow \text{Solution-focused sessions (typically 1–8 visits).} \\
\textbf{Stage 4: Referral (if required)} &\rightarrow \text{Long-term therapy, psychiatrist, rehab, financial/legal expert.} \\
\textbf{Stage 5: Follow-up \& Closure} &\rightarrow \text{After 30/60/90 days, ensure stability, close case.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Analysis of EAP ROI Calculation

> [!NOTE] **Worked Numerical Example (Frequently Asked in Numerical HRM Questions)**
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A company employs **1,000 employees**. Last year the company spent **₹5,00,000** on its EAP. After one year of EAP operation, the following improvements were observed:
> - Absenteeism reduced by **800 days** (costing **₹1,500/day** in lost productivity).
> - Turnover reduced by **15 employees** (average replacement cost per employee **₹3,00,000**).
> - Medical insurance claims reduced by **₹2,00,000**.
>
> Calculate the **ROI of the EAP**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Total Savings (Benefits Generated)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings from reduced absenteeism} &= 800 \text{ days} \times \text{Rs. } 1500/\text{day} \\
&= \text{Rs. } 12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Savings from Reduced Turnover**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Turnover savings} &= 15 \text{ employees} \times \text{Rs. } 3{,}00{,}000/\text{employee} \\
&= \text{Rs. } 45{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Savings from Reduced Medical Claims**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Medical savings} &= \text{Rs. } 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Savings} &= 12{,}00{,}000 + 45{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 59{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Benefit (Savings − Cost)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= 59{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 54{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Return on Investment (ROI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{EAP Cost}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{54{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 1080 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**
> For every **₹1 invested** in the EAP, the company saved approximately **₹10.80 in tangible costs**. This is consistent with the global industry benchmark of **₹3 to ₹6 saved per ₹1 invested**, indicating an exceptionally high-performing programme — possibly because the pre-EAP baseline was very poor.

## 3.4 EAP Utilisation Rate — Worked Example

**Problem:** A firm has **2,500 employees**. During the year, **175 unique employees** used the EAP at least once. Compute the **EAP Utilisation Rate**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Utilisation Rate} &= \dfrac{175}{2500} \times 100 \\
&= 7 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Industry Benchmarks for Utilisation**
> - Below **3 %** — Under-utilised; awareness is poor; communication must be intensified.
> - **3 % to 8 %** — Healthy range (most global firms).
> - Above **8 %** — Excellent; indicates strong trust and effective communication.
> - Above **15 %** — Investigate; may indicate severe workplace stressors (red flag).

## 3.5 Common Pitfalls in EAP Design (Analytical Insights)

> [!WARNING] **Why EAPs Fail (KTU Examiner's Insight)**
> 1. **Confidentiality breach** — Single breach destroys trust permanently.
> 2. **Stigma** — Employees fear being labelled "weak" or "problematic."
> 3. **Poor awareness** — 40 % of employees in Indian surveys do not know whether their firm has an EAP.
> 4. **Manager referral failure** — Managers may "punish" instead of "refer" troubled employees.
> 5. **Vendor SLA gaps** — Slow response time, language barriers, poor counsellor quality.
> 6. **Lack of evaluation** — No ROI tracking means management loses interest after 2 years.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define Employee Assistance Programme (EAP). Mention any two objectives of an EAP.**
**[KTU University Exam - Module 4 - Short Answer Pattern]**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
An **Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)** is a confidential, employer-sponsored workplace programme that helps employees identify and resolve personal, behavioural, health, or work-related problems that adversely affect their job performance and well-being. **(2 marks)**

**Two objectives:**
1. To provide **early intervention and confidential counselling** so that personal issues do not escalate into performance problems. **(0.5 marks)**
2. To **reduce absenteeism, turnover, and healthcare costs** of the organisation, thereby improving overall productivity. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List any four guiding principles of a successful EAP.**
**[KTU University Exam - Module 4 - Short Answer Pattern]**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
1. **Confidentiality** — Information disclosed is kept strictly private. **(0.75 marks)**
2. **Voluntariness** — Employees opt in voluntarily. **(0.75 marks)**
3. **Accessibility** — Services are available 24×7 and in multiple modes (call, online, in-person). **(0.75 marks)**
4. **Professionalism** — Delivered by qualified, licensed counsellors. **(0.75 marks)**

*(Alternate answers accepted: Neutrality, Anonymity, Continuity, Non-discriminatory access.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - End Semester Pattern - Module 4 Choice-Based Question]**
> *Answer any ONE of the following:*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**Q: Explain in detail the concept, objectives, types, and benefits of an Employee Assistance Programme (EAP).**

**(a)** Discuss the **objectives of an EAP** from the perspective of employees, employer, and society. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **different types / models of EAPs** with their merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Objectives of an EAP (7 marks)**

An Employee Assistance Programme is designed to serve three stakeholder groups:

**1. Objectives for the Employee (2 marks):**
- Provides confidential professional help at **no cost** for personal problems.
- Protects the employee from job loss arising from treatable personal issues.
- Promotes **work-life balance** and emotional well-being.
- Offers access to specialist resources (psychiatrist, lawyer, financial planner) that the employee may not be able to afford independently.

**2. Objectives for the Employer (3 marks):**
- Enhances **productivity** by addressing factors that impair performance.
- Reduces **absenteeism, presenteeism, and tardiness** — major cost drivers.
- Reduces **employee turnover** and replacement cost (typically 1.5× to 2× annual salary).
- Reduces **medical and insurance** expenditure linked to stress, alcohol, and lifestyle diseases.
- Helps comply with legal obligations (POSH Act, Mental Healthcare Act 2017, Factories Act, ISO 45001).

**3. Objectives for the Society (2 marks):**
- Strengthens families and reduces social pathologies.
- Contributes to **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)**.
- Builds a healthier, more stable national workforce.

**(b) Types of EAPs (7 marks)**

| Model | Description | Merits | Demerits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Internal EAP** | In-house counsellors employed by the firm | Cultural familiarity, onsite access, lower long-term cost | Confidentiality risk, limited specialisation, stigma |
| **External EAP** | Outsourced to a third-party vendor | High confidentiality, 24×7 access, multi-lingual, specialised | Higher per-employee cost, vendor dependency, less cultural fit |
| **Blended / Hybrid** | Mix of internal staff and external specialists | Best of both worlds | Coordination complexity, needs clear protocols |
| **Consortium EAP** | Multiple SMEs pool resources in a cluster | Lowest cost, good for industrial clusters | Slower response, cross-employer confidentiality risk |
| **Member-Assistance / Peer-Based** | Trained peer volunteers act as first responders | High trust, cultural sensitivity | Limited expertise, boundary violation risk |

*Conclusion (1 mark):* Most Indian firms prefer **external EAPs** for confidentiality and cost-effectiveness, while large IT firms use **hybrid models** to balance trust and specialisation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**Q: Describe the step-by-step process of designing and implementing an Employee Assistance Programme. Also explain how its effectiveness can be evaluated.**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step process of implementing an EAP** in an organisation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **methods of evaluating the effectiveness of an EAP**, including ROI calculation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Step-by-Step Implementation of an EAP (7 marks)**

1. **Need Assessment (1 mark):** Conduct employee surveys, focus groups, exit interviews, and analyse absenteeism/turnover data to confirm the need.

2. **Top Management Commitment (0.5 marks):** Secure budget, written policy, and visible CEO endorsement — without this, the EAP will fail.

3. **Policy Formulation (1 mark):** Draft a formal EAP policy covering objectives, scope, eligibility, confidentiality clause, and roles.

4. **Vendor Selection / Model Choice (0.5 marks):** Decide between internal, external, blended, or consortium model; issue RFP, evaluate vendors, sign SLA.

5. **Service Package Design (0.5 marks):** Define helpline, e-counselling, face-to-face sessions, manager training, awareness workshops.

6. **Communication & Awareness (1 mark):** Launch campaign using posters, e-mailers, induction modules, intranet portal; emphasise confidentiality.

7. **Training of Managers & Peer Volunteers (0.5 marks):** Train them in identification, referral, and de-stigmatisation — they are the gatekeepers.

8. **Service Delivery (1 mark):** Begin intake → assessment → counselling → referral → follow-up as per SOPs.

9. **Monitoring (0.5 marks):** Quarterly aggregated MIS reports (no individual data).

10. **Evaluation & Continuous Improvement (0.5 marks):** Annual ROI study, employee feedback, course-correction.

**(b) Evaluating EAP Effectiveness (7 marks)**

Evaluation can be conducted at **four levels (Donaldson's Four-Level Model)**:

| Level | What is Measured | Tools |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Level 1 — Reaction** | Employee satisfaction with EAP | Feedback forms, surveys |
| **Level 2 — Learning** | Knowledge / awareness gained | Pre-post awareness tests |
| **Level 3 — Behaviour** | Change in workplace behaviour | Manager observations, supervisor reports |
| **Level 4 — Results** | Tangible business outcomes | Absenteeism, turnover, medical claims, accidents, ROI |

**Key Metrics (2 marks):**
- **Utilisation Rate** = (Users ÷ Eligible Employees) × 100; healthy range = 3 % to 8 %.
- **ROI** = (Total Savings − EAP Cost) ÷ EAP Cost × 100; industry benchmark = ₹3 to ₹6 saved per ₹1 spent.
- **Absenteeism Delta, Turnover Delta, Medical Claim Delta.**

**Other Evaluation Tools:** Employee satisfaction surveys, focus groups, EAP vendor's annual report, comparative analysis with pre-EAP baseline data, third-party audits.

*Conclusion (1 mark):* A good EAP must be **continuously evaluated**; otherwise utilisation drops, management loses interest, and the programme becomes a "white elephant."

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Lose Marks in EAP Questions**
> 1. **Confusing EAP with "Employee Welfare"** — Welfare is statutory, broad, and physical (canteen, transport, crèche). EAP is **confidential, voluntary, professional, problem-focused**. Mixing them = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing "Counselling" alone as an answer** — Counselling is only **one component** of EAP. You must mention **assessment, referral, crisis intervention, evaluation, and awareness** to score full marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Confidentiality** — The single most important principle. If you don't mention it, the examiner will deduct at least **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping Types/Models** — A common 7-mark sub-question expects at least **4 models** with merits & demerits in tabular form.
> 5. **No ROI Numerical Practice** — KTU has started asking short numericals. Memorise the formula:
>
> $$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Savings} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$$
> 6. **Stating the Origin Year Wrongly** — Origin is **1940s** in the USA, not 1980s. Many students write "EAPs started in 1990s" — that is when they came to India.
> 7. **Writing about POSH Act as if it is the same as EAP** — POSH is for sexual harassment only; EAP covers a much wider 8-category scope.

---

> [!NOTE] **KTU Module 4 — Employee Assistance Programmes: One-Page Recap**
> - **EAP** = confidential, voluntary, employer-sponsored problem-solving programme.
> - **Origin**: 1940s USA, Occupational Alcoholism Programmes; expanded to 8 problem areas today.
> - **Origin in India**: 1990s, IT/ITeS firms.
> - **8 Problem Categories**: Alcohol, drugs, mental health, marital/family, financial, legal, workplace conflict, health/lifestyle.
> - **7 Cardinal Principles**: Confidentiality, Voluntariness, Accessibility, Professionalism, Neutrality, Anonymity, Continuity.
> - **5 Models**: Internal, External, Blended, Consortium, Member-Assistance/Peer.
> - **6 Components**: Awareness, Assessment, Counselling, Referral, Crisis Intervention, Evaluation.
> - **10-Step Implementation**: Need Assessment → Top Management Buy-in → Policy → Vendor → Service Design → Awareness → Training → Delivery → Monitoring → Evaluation.
> - **5-Stage Counselling Pathway**: Intake → Assessment → Short-term Counselling → Referral → Follow-up & Closure.
> - **Evaluation Model**: Donaldson's 4-Level Model (Reaction, Learning, Behaviour, Results).
> - **Key Formulae**:
>   - $\text{Utilisation Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Unique Users}}{\text{Eligible Employees}} \times 100$ — Healthy range **3 % to 8 %**.
>   - $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Savings} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ — Industry benchmark **₹3 to ₹6 per ₹1**.
>   - $\text{Turnover Cost} \approx 1.5\times \text{ to } 2.0\times \text{Annual Salary}$.
> - **Benefits**: Quadruple — Employees, Employer, HR, Society.
> - **Indian Laws Interlinked**: POSH Act 2013, Mental Healthcare Act 2017, Factories Act 1948, ISO 45001.
> - **Common Pitfall**: Never equate EAP with Employee Welfare — they are fundamentally different in scope, confidentiality, and intent.
> - **Most-Favoured KTU Question Type**: *"Explain the objectives / types / process / benefits / effectiveness of EAP"* — usually a 7-mark or 14-mark structured essay.
